questions.question: What is the primary benefit of loop unrolling?
options: It reduces the total number of instructions in the program
It reduces branch overhead and exposes instruction-level parallelism by duplicating the loop body
It converts loops into recursive function calls
It guarantees the loop will be vectorized
questions.explanation: Loop unrolling duplicates the body N times so the branch instruction executes N times less frequently. It also creates more independent instructions that the CPU can execute in parallel via superscalar execution. Trade-off: larger code size.

questions.question: A loop has a[i] = a[i-1] + 1. Why can't this be vectorized?
options: The addition operation is too expensive
There is a loop-carried flow dependence — iteration i depends on the result from iteration i-1
The array is too small to benefit from vectorization
The compiler doesn't support vectorizing addition
questions.explanation: This is a flow (RAW) dependence: iteration i reads the value written by iteration i-1. SIMD requires all iterations to execute simultaneously, but here each iteration must wait for the previous result. This is a serial recurrence that cannot be parallelized.

questions.question: Loop interchange improves performance primarily by improving what?
options: Instruction count
Register allocation
Cache locality — ensuring the inner loop traverses contiguous memory
Branch prediction accuracy
questions.explanation: When a 2D array is stored in row-major order, iterating row-by-row in the inner loop accesses contiguous memory (stride-1), hitting cache lines efficiently. Interchanging the loops so the contiguous dimension is the inner loop improves cache utilization dramatically (2-5x speedup is typical).

questions.question: What width of SIMD does AVX2 operate on for single-precision floats?
options: 2 floats (64-bit)
4 floats (128-bit)
8 floats (256-bit)
16 floats (512-bit)
questions.explanation: AVX2 provides 256-bit registers (YMM) that hold 8 single-precision (32-bit) floats or 4 double-precision (64-bit) floats. SSE operates on 128-bit (4 floats), and AVX-512 on 512-bit (16 floats).

questions.question: What is loop vectorization?
options: Converting loops into recursive functions
Transforming scalar loop iterations into SIMD operations that process multiple elements per instruction
Unrolling loops to reduce branch overhead
Moving loop-invariant code outside the loop
questions.explanation: Vectorization transforms a loop like `for(i) C[i] = A[i] + B[i]` into SIMD instructions that add 4/8/16 elements at once. The compiler must prove that iterations are independent (no data dependencies between iterations) and that memory accesses are aligned.
